topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure for the new city practically mocked me from the coffee table. Liam was already packing boxes, his laughter echoing in the apartment as he meticulously taped up his favorite vinyl collection. I watched him, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs, as he spoke excitedly about the new opportunities. "It’s going to be incredible," he beamed, his eyes shining with a light I suddenly felt excluded from. I fiddled with the edge of my worn sweater, the fabric suddenly rough against my skin. Every shared inside joke, every late-night study session we’d built over years, felt suddenly hollow, a collection of memories being neatly boxed up for storage. I tried to sound casual when I asked him about the specifics of the move, but the pitch of my voice cracked slightly. He just waved a dismissive hand, already engrossed in planning the logistics.

[story 2]

I stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. The silence in the room was loud, amplifying the frantic rhythm of my own pulse. Seeing Liam’s excitement—the genuine, unburdened joy he felt about this massive leap—made a strange, hot pressure build behind my sternum. It wasn't happiness for him that made my stomach clench; it was the sudden, sharp realization that a significant piece of my established world was shifting without me. I picked up my phone, intending to text him a congratulatory message, but the words dissolved before they reached my fingertips. Instead, I paced the floor, my footsteps quick and uneven, the energy trapped inside me demanding an outlet. I needed to do something, anything, to anchor myself before the whole landscape of our friendship dissolved into distance.

[story 3]

"So, are you really doing this?" I managed, my voice sounding thin and reedy, like a poorly tuned instrument. Liam turned, his expression warm and reassuring, the very picture of someone ready to conquer the unknown. But when he launched into the detailed itinerary, mapping out new neighborhoods and potential haunts, I found myself leaning back against the doorframe, arms crossed tightly over my chest. A strange tightness constricted my throat. I focused intently on a dust mote dancing in a sunbeam, trying to filter the overwhelming sensation bubbling up—a deep, unsettling feeling of being left behind on the starting line. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and mumbled something about being excited, acutely aware of how much I wanted to pull him back, to demand that this grand adventure pause just long enough for me to process the sudden emptiness settling in my chest.
